name: Ely
short_description: Rockhound near the Boundary Waters in northeastern Minnesota's Superior National Forest area. Search for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in glacially-carved terrain.
description:
Ely sits in the heart of Minnesota's mineral-rich Superior National Forest region, where Archean-age metamorphic bedrock has been exposed and scattered by glacial activity. This area is part of the Canadian Shield's eastern extension, where ancient granite and gneiss formations contain abundant quartz, feldspar, and mica crystals. Rockhounds visit Ely primarily to explore public lands and forest service areas around the Boundary Waters Canoe Area, where glacial outwash deposits and exposed bedrock offer accessible collecting opportunities.

The flat to gently rolling terrain around Ely makes this beginner-friendly compared to more remote Superior National Forest locations. Most productive collecting occurs along stream beds, glacial deposits, and exposed rock outcrops where weathering has loosened mineral specimens. The area's proximity to the Boundary Waters means access is generally open on BLM-managed and national forest lands, though specific parcels require verification.

Rockhounders here typically focus on crystal pockets in granite outcrops and secondary quartz specimens weathered from bedrock. Feldspar is common in feldspathic pegmatites, and mica flakes appear throughout glacial till and stream deposits. The region's long winters mean best collecting is May through September, with access sometimes limited by snowmelt in spring.

Locations around Ely vary in accessibility: some roadside pullouts allow parking near exposed rock faces, while others require short hikes into national forest land. Always verify current access status with the Superior National Forest office in Ely before collecting, as regulations can change seasonally and by management area.
history: This area lies within the Superior Province of the Canadian Shield, where Archean granitic and metamorphic rock formed over 2.7 billion years ago. Glacial activity during the Pleistocene epoch scoured this landscape, fractured bedrock, and deposited mineral-rich till throughout northeastern Minnesota, creating the excellent quartz and feldspar specimens found in surface deposits today.
tips: Search along stream beds and glacial outwash areas where erosion has concentrated heavier minerals and loosened crystal specimens from surrounding bedrock. Look for exposed granite and gneiss outcrops on hillsides and roadcuts, where weathering has opened small crystal pockets filled with quartz points and feldspar crystals. Bring a cold chisel and small hammer to carefully extract specimens from rock faces without damaging surrounding material. Visit after spring snowmelt when streams are running and have freshly exposed mineral deposits on their banks.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain typical of glacially-carved landscape. Elevation around 1,400 feet. Terrain includes exposed granite and gneiss bedrock, glacial till deposits, stream beds, and mixed boreal forest vegetation (spruce, pine, birch). Most accessible collecting areas are within short walking distance (under 1 mile) from parking pullouts. Footing can be uneven over exposed rock and glacial erratics.
best_season: May - September. Winter snow and ice make access difficult October through April. Late spring and early summer offer the best combination of accessible terrain and freshly exposed mineral deposits in stream beds after snowmelt.
written_directions: From Ely, take County Road 44 east or explore forest service roads branching from MN 169 within Superior National Forest boundaries near Morse Township. Several pullouts and informal parking areas exist along these roads near exposed rock outcrops and stream crossings. Consult Superior National Forest maps for specific access points and current road conditions, as seasonal closures affect backcountry routes. GPS: 47.875586, -91.914491 marks the general Ely area center; specific collecting sites vary within a 5-10 mile radius.
road_conditions: Mixed paved and unpaved forest service roads. Most primary routes passable by standard passenger vehicles spring through fall. Some secondary roads may have washboard or ruts; high-clearance helpful but not essential. Verify current conditions with Superior National Forest office.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and small parking areas scattered along forest service roads near rockhounding sites. Capacity typically 2-6 vehicles per location. No developed parking lots at most mineral collecting areas.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA guidelines. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect Boundary Waters Canoe Area restrictions where applicable. Verify specific management area regulations with Superior National Forest office in Ely before collecting.
rules:
Personal use collection only
25 lbs maximum per day
No commercial collection permitted
No motorized equipment for digging or excavation
Respect posted boundaries and closure signs
Verify current regulations with Superior National Forest office
nearest_services: Ely, approximately 5-15 miles from most collecting sites. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging. Superior National Forest office in Ely provides current access and regulation information.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved roads to many parking areas are passable by standard vehicles May through September. Walking distances to collecting sites typically under 1 mile, but terrain is uneven with exposed rock and glacial boulders. Sturdy footwear recommended. No wheelchair access at remote mineral sites.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can I find rockhounding near Ely, Minnesota?
faq.answer: The Ely area is best for collecting quartz, feldspar, mica, and tourmaline specimens. These minerals are common in the Archean granite and metamorphic bedrock exposed throughout Superior National Forest. Quartz often appears as clear or milky crystals in small pockets, feldspar forms larger cleavable pieces, and mica flakes are abundant in glacial till and stream deposits.

faq.question: Is rockhounding near Ely free or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding on Superior National Forest public lands near Ely is free and does not require a permit. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use without commercial intent. Always verify current access status with the Superior National Forest office in Ely, as some management areas may have seasonal restrictions.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound around Ely?
faq.answer: May through September is the optimal season for rockhounding near Ely. Winter snow and ice make access difficult from October through April. Late spring offers the best conditions with freshly exposed mineral deposits in streams after snowmelt and accessible terrain throughout the area.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding near Ely, Minnesota?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are allowed on most Superior National Forest public lands around Ely. However, check specific area regulations as some Boundary Waters-adjacent lands have restrictions. Keep dogs under control and respect wildlife areas, particularly during nesting season in spring and early summer.

faq.question: Is rockhounding near Ely suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Ely is excellent for beginners and families. The terrain is relatively flat and accessible, parking areas are close to collecting sites, and the variety of minerals means you can find specimens even without experience. Children ages 6 and up can participate in most areas. Bring basic tools like a cold chisel and small hammer, wear sturdy shoes, and start near stream beds where minerals are easiest to spot.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Ely?
faq.answer: A cold chisel and small hammer are essential for extracting quartz and feldspar specimens from rock outcrops. Bring a backpack to carry specimens (limit 25 lbs daily), a field guide to identify minerals, sturdy hiking boots for uneven terrain, and water. A GPS device or offline map helps locate productive areas. May through September weather is mild, but always bring rain gear.

meta_title: Ely Minnesota Rockhounding - Find Quartz & Feldspar
meta_description: Rockhound near Ely, MN for quartz, feldspar, and mica on Superior National Forest public lands. Free collecting, beginner-friendly, year-round access. Plan your trip.
